speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Dick Benge had never worked in woods when his parents came to Princeton from Nebraska in 1913, but he's never really stopped working in woods since. He has sharp recollections of events involving people in the woods that show what the logger's life was like. Gambling, moonshining, IWW activity, and the authorities' attitudes towards them bring stories to mind. So does the Depression, and fires, and thoughts of the kind of man the lumberjack was. Noted characters like Pat Malone, Dick Farrell, T .P. Jones, and Potlatch Joe are some of Dick's acquaintances who figure in the fun.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: First thing I want to ask you is do you know why your folks decided to come out here from Nebraska?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Yes, I do. You know, Dad had poor health. And the doctor told him he'd better come to a higher climate. We lived at that time in Nebraska. And that's how he come to come out here. We all moved out on Dad's calendar. We helped. Well, he lived several years after he came out here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: After he came out here and gotten his higher climate, why? Why did he pick Idaho? Why did he pick Idaho?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well, now that's another question because there's other families that come from right in there that we knew that lived here at Princeton. McFarland's was one of them. Baines. McVeigh's was another one. We knew them there. So it's just like, you know, moving into the only one that's been raised together in Missouri was the 80's. So that's how that come.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you remember from when you were a kid back there? What it was like farming in Nebraska and in Missouri?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: It was, I don't remember farming in Missouri, but Nebraska, it was, well, dry. I had, the grasshoppers was pretty hard and when the grasshoppers didn't get you sometimes the hare storms would. Just the way it was. Boy, I'll tell you, man, that was a hard country farming. Sometimes you raised good cops, sometimes you didn't raise much.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How do you think it compared with out here?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Oh, this is all together a different country. All together different from what it was. You know, we had the wind, lightning storms, hail storms, everything. Boy, I tell you, you know, we come out here, we hadn't been here very long. Elton Cripple, that was Herschel's brother, they lived right close to us there and come up with a little windstorm and blow it down a tree or two. That was before it was all done.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: They come up after the windstorm was over and talk about how the wind blows. We didn't notice it was blowing. Because the wind blowed that hard every day there. We had to clap a few of thunder, you know, and we didn't take much anything but that. They come up and they're talking about it and we told them, well, that's not, we didn't notice. It was just yarching. So we told Shorty and Herschel both. They were talking about why that four or five were short lightning flashes and claps of thunder. We told them, we said, you ought to go back to Nebraska and Iowa and Missouri and through that country. You're going to see a lightning storm. And I told Shorty, I said, Elton, I said, I've seen a lightning there, you're almost reading newspaper by it. No, he said, well, I said, all right. So he went back to Iowa, him and his cousin on a visit there, some relatives from Iowa. He come back and he come up and he said, Dick, I apologize. Well, he apologized in fact. He said, I thought you was lying about lightning, but he said we had one while we was there. And he said, I figured you didn't give it quite strong enough.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: So, man, they did have some storms there, you know. Lightning and thunder, you know. It was. I seen it almost just wasn't flash right after another one. But I thought, you know, just upset the house and everything. What we all used to. We never paid no attention.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember anything about the trip out here?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Oh, yeah. I come out here on crutches when I come.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You was on crutches?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Yeah, horse fell on me and broke my leg and pulled the place. I was on crutches when we come out here. We left Wymore, Nebraska and we come to Lincoln and then we got on the Great Northern. Well, we come from Wymore up to Beatty's on the Burlington. Then they put us on the Great Northern. Well, there was three coaches of us. They just took the same coaches and a quarter he come through and he said, you're all going to have to move back in another car. So we asked him why. Well, he said, you're going right through. This said, this three coaches are going through to Seattle. And he said, I'll be on at all times. And he said, you'll move back there and they said, you won't be bothered anymore. Changing cars or nothing because they said we're going to keep them. Right on the same train all time. Just take the three coaches and put them on the other line and go on. That was pretty nice. So we come right through to Spokane from Lincoln, Nebraska right on the same, in the same coach.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So what happened after you got off?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well, we got off in Spokane and we were only there. It seemed to me like, near as I can remember, about an hour and then we come through since they don't like. We come through down here and if they don't like it, then we come up next day at noon on the WINM. That's the one that runs up through here. We went in, you know, we've been used to them potatoes there in Nebraska. They was all dried potatoes, you know, boy strong. Well, I thought they'd sugared the potatoes the first night there for supper. Yeah, they tasted sweet and nice. We all talked about why they put the sugar in the potatoes first. Don't find out, they're salt sugared. Yeah, because them potatoes there, oh boy, they were strong, you know, dry weather. Not irrigated, nothing, they didn't know what irrigating was there. Boy, I'll tell a man they were strong. Green, you know, you know what a green potato is. You know, when he's green he's strong. Yeah, no, we had a nice trip.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So how did you come about to locate here in this place?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well, here we, I'll tell you how. We come into Princeton and, Eldon Tribbles, Herschel's brother, his wife had just passed away. And he had a place down below and he had to sell it, so dad bought it. Off of him, right across the road from where Herschel is now. Well, he bought the place off of him, and they stayed right there and my mother and they both passed away. Right down the place they bought off of Tribble, well, my boy owns it now. Yeah, he owned the acre there, right. The way it all come about.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there a place there when you got there, a building?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Oh yeah, we had a little log house and one thing or another, during the good spring, which helped, an old log barn.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: And you were getting around on crutches. And you were getting around on crutches. No, I was getting around on crutches. I was going to hurt you in January and I never got off my crutches until my neighbor. I broke them. Because he told me, he said, that leg is broke before it breaks, you got to be careful with it. You got to let all that bone set before you start. So he told me what to do. So after about so long, I took my knife and took the taffs off myself. He told me just what I could take it off myself.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did you bust it? Huh? How did you bust it?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Horse fell on me. Horse fell on this leg and I had a steel stirrup. And here's what happened. It twisted my foot. See, that steel stirrup. Of course, I had been, had a horse, a giant or two, to fall on there. I don't know, it's the first time, I don't know what happened. But anyhow, I kept, I thought the stirrup was straight. But it wasn't, it rolled and it rolled. So I took my knife and took my taffs off. Bucked this heel right off right up through here to the joint. And broke this little joint out here. On my little, little one out. I broke it in two and bust this knuckle here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Boy, I tell you man, it was nice, didn't it?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: And there we had a very good doctor because, well I tell you, he was the doctor there in Mymoi. He was head physician there for the Burlington Railroad. He had a regular car there. He had a wreck, he had to go. And they took him right in the car. Well that's the end. Of course we knowed him and we called him up and he come right out and said it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did your folks move in pretty easily? Was it hard to make a change when they got out here?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Nah. We knowed too many people that was from the east right there. Well that and them was all raised together. Lots of them, see. Well, they fit right in, you know. Made it nice.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So when did you first start working in the woods?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: That fall. After I got around to work a little last summer and then I took in trash then. That fall. And for one of the fellas that we knewed in the rest, he had a trash machine so I knocked him. My father, Dane. Dane, of course I knowed them all there. And then that fall, after thrashing was over, we come in and we had timber there on the place. So, that he wasn't very, well he couldn't get around, he couldn't work much. So I hired a man to help me. And I talked to the log, skidded them, decked them out, and got them ready for a flail all that winter.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Had you ever worked in the woods before that?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: No, there was no woods there to work in. That's what I figured. I was going to the prairie, you know, so I had to learn it all. And I started then working in the woods, you know. Well the next year, I went back out thrashing prairie. And I come in along that winter again down there on the play hall. And the next spring, my Herschel, he went to Bowville. And I went up there and I worked up in the woods and worked in the woods every time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember what it was like for you to learn? What the learning, some of the things you had to figure out?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Yes, but here's something about any job you've got. If you take the interest in it, you learn faster.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you know that?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: And if you don't take that big interest in it, well, forget it. I don't care much about it. Go on about something else. But it interested me, logging. And I kept right on. Well, I had done everything in the woods. There's only one thing that I never did do. Drive a logging truck I didn't want to do. Never wanted to drive a logging truck. But I drove four horses on a sleigh and everything else. But a logging truck was on the mountain. But I unloaded logs here in Potlatch off of trucks for ten years. They'd bring me down out of the woods to unload trucks. And I unloaded them with a PB then.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Can you remember, if you go back to the first times that you were logging, can you remember how you ran the operation then?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: You'd go out and learn it. I got caught a time or two. I got broke up a time or two in the woods. But you'd carefully start. I don't care. You're going to get caught sometimes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many guys was doing it? How many guys was doing it?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well, when we started logging down there, Dad and I, and I got another man to help us, and then him and I, we skidded decked the logs from that winter. We sleigh hoarded them. Just the two of us. He just had one little team. We got us a nice little logging class. Just got in what we could get in. We had all the money. Wasn't nothin' on money. Now, I tripled down there. He went and put a big coo in. He come up there after we got done logging.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He said, how come? I said, how come? What do you mean?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: He said, you fellas made more money than I did. Just went in and said, well, maybe we did. I don't know. I said, what we got us ours. We ain't got nothin' for help. Speak of. I figured I'd give him two and a half a day for helpin' me sow logs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I said, what?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: I was skiddin' them all and decked them myself.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: That's the way I learned.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well, then when I went to the boat, I worked there with Eldon Treble, Herschel's brother in the kitchen. And then I went out of that and went to scalin'. And I scaled a while, and then I come down. Worked down here almost a year in the woods. Then I went back up because they shut this down again. This tank they had down below here, two, a mile and a half. Had a good loggin' tank out down there, just same as the one over here in the flat. They shut that down, and then I went back up and went to work in the woods. Went back up that time, went into the donkey business. Steamed donkeys. Well, I showed you a picture of one of them steamed donkeys. It was old donkey work done up there.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Then they went out of that and went into horses again. And then they went out of that and went into the caterpillar. Then they went back into horses again. And, well, I don't know who was running the layout. Then finally they went into truckin'. Caterpillar and truckin'. Well, it's just got bigger and it's been that way ever since. But the horses and the railroad was the cheapest loggin' they ever done. I know, I was right here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why? Why?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well, let's put it this way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: If you got a truckin' road up here in the wintertime, how much is it gonna cost you to keep that road up?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well, if you got a railroad out there, of course, a railroad's expensive, I'll admit, but the way they done it, you know, it wasn't too bad because they had a steam shovel. And they built an upgrade, they built a mile a grade a day to lay the track on. Well, then they had a gang of twelve, they'd come along, and they laid a mile a steel a day. See, well, after they got that laid, we'll put it this way. After they got that laid, two or three men would keep that whole layout up. Well, you take a truck road, look at the riggin', look what it cost you to keep that road up.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Yeah, that's it. Yeah. Yeah, that's the whole set up right there. And here one time, you know, they had, well, about 42 mile a steel laid in here. Railroad track. They had a log on it, and they used twenty teams all the time. Of course, we put in our logs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When did they start doing that in here?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Oh, let's see. I believe it was in twenty,

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: about nineteen, nineteen I think, or somewhere along there. They put the first camp in here and started building.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And how long did the thing go on?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well, they moved out in twenty -seven. Twenty -seven, twenty -eight. But they kept twenty teams in here and kept them busy all the time. This is the same camp right across the road. Well, now before, I'll tell you what happened. I was working up there at old camp two, above Bowell, when I was talking to Logan, and he was coming down, and come find out they couldn't get to camp two through the railroad bridge at Harvard. If it was too wide, you wouldn't come through the railroad bridge. They built a turnaround, they wanted these logs out here, so they turned around and built a ground camp. Down here about a mile and a quarter, they built just a whole ground camp. Well, them, while they were doing that, we'd log in there, then they built that camp that I just showed you here. It's pot -like. Well, they built it so it'd go through the railroad bridge. Well, then when they got down here, well, then they took it over there on, they took the same camps that we used here, they took it over there on Flattrick, because it'd get through the bridge.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So it was all to get through the bridge?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: They built one that could go through the bridge, you see. They never thought about it till. Well, they were all ready to move and everything, and old Bob Jones each come down here and go to logs, and a boy and a noggle -y come along and he said, we can't do that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: He said, if we're going to do that, we're going to have to tear that bridge down at Harvard, he said, to get it through there. He won't go through.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How do you know?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: He said, it's down in Maynard.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well, it was a good thing they didn't get it done before, he's come in Maynard. He's a little shy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When they was logging in here, did they take everything, or did they just take the big stuff, or what?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Oh, they took everything. Everything. I'll tell you one thing that did happen while it was at it, why, Japan bought a lot of timber off of them.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: So Jack Donovan was a check -sciller, and he come down one day, and of course he was a check -sciller when I was scaling up there, and he said, don't take that in.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I said, what's the matter with him?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: He said, that's a called log.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, how do you figure that, Jack?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well, he said, sit down on this log now, I explained it. He said, you know, we're selling a lot of timber to Japan. And he said, they're getting it over on the coast. And he said, they're putting it aboard the boat, cheaper than we can sell it and ship it there. Well, what it was, it was all big, big timber just squared up. And they take it to Japan, you know, and they whipsaw it. One gets the bow, one blows, and I whip her out the hand. They just took her over there and sunk her, and that's why they raised her up and stole her up. Bam! So, they left some good timber up here, but made good wood.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well,

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: you know, it's this way, you know. I was down there at the Sun, you know, he worked for Simpson, not Redwood. But, uh, what he's been doing there now for Simpson, I guess, about,

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: about 20 years, I guess, 19, 20 years, worked right there at that one place. For old Simpson, and we was down there this spring, and I was talking to him, and I was talking to the boss, and they'd been kicking on Simpson and Japan logs.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: And this fellow said to me, he said, I don't see how we can stop it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I said, how do you mean?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well, he said they just come over here with $21 ,800 ,000 bought land timber and all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He said, how are we going to keep them from handling their own timber?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well, that's the way this big business is going.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, how long ago was this? This was in the 20s that they were shipping to Japan too, right?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Why, sure. I bet. Nothing new.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Nothing new?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Why, no, they were shipping to Japan during World War I.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Yeah, they were shipping over there then. Shipping all of that out of Cumberland 1, of course. At that time, they didn't ship them logs, they shipped them tans. They squared them up and tanted them out and shipped them over there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So, how are you going to stop it?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: We can't stop it. It was just like this way, you know. My wife was cooking over there at the University of Moscow, and it was, it got pretty well acquainted with him. He came over here and his lady friend, that's the boy he's married, he came over here and he was over here several different times. So we got a letter from him this Christmas. And he's 35 miles south of the equator. Well, it's the way that big money outfit operates. And he told us, he said, I'm driving a helicopter. I'm following it, big one.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And he said, warehouses?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: He said, tell Dick, he said, that warehousers owns 85 percent of it. And he said, the army here on this island owns the other 15 percent. Yeah. Well, now this fellow that the boy works for there in California, that Simpson, warehouses are Hems related and they work together. Got two big mills there, bigger than that in Lewiston, and just highway run between them. I don't know if they want to load logs one, they take it there, we'll drive one and load logs the other, and they take it there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So, what are you going to do? But, when he went in the island, the warehouses went to the islands, what did Simpson do?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: He moved through three mills into Alaska.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You get the idea? They got it on both ends, didn't they?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: That's just the way it works.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: That's the way it works, it goes. Well, you know when all the scaling up there, I worked there at the mill. Oh, there's a picture, I'll show it to you.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Here.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: I worked there at this mill 19 years, well in fact, there was three sawmills there.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: I'll show you how it is. I see here, is the Milwaukee railroad, right down through here. Here's the sawmill, here's the sawmill, here's the sawmill, and here's the sawmill here, and here's the planer. And this is saw logs here, and that's lumber.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Now this bear heard from Portland, he started all that operation. Furnished in Montyford. But now it's all tore up. But, we got the mill here, I didn't get it better on the grasses. But, they don't saw any more lumber, they saw chips. The Scott Paper Company got it. And the mill sits here, and they just cut the chips and blow them in here. Now, here's something else, you know. Weyerhaelder was going to be able to put in a shipper at Carsey. Because this outfitter here didn't handle big enough logs. So we said, alright, we'll put in a shipper in Carsey to handle it. And, I'll get that back up there. And he said, you really don't want, we said we want to take our chips through it. Alright, so now, I was talking to one of the boys the other day, and what they're doing now. They run eight hours for Scott Paper Company. And the other eight hours they run for Weyerhaelder. And they're holding them chips through, and what Weyerhaelder gets. By truck.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: So I guess Weyerhaelder made them come to time, you know. Oh, they can do it.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When they logged the country off here, did they then turn around and sell it for farms?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Yeah. Yeah, that's what they done. I bought all this. I bought this one time here, I had 240 acres. And this 40 here, and that and that, and that. I give 800 for this one. 700 for this one, 800 for that one. Back on the hill over there, I got that for 600. And I bought a 40 right over the hill for 400. And that's the way they cut it out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So that way you didn't have to clear it, it was cleared for you pretty much, huh?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: No, I had to clear it. Because, well, some days we had to, well, we didn't have no dozers or nothing, we had to do all the ham. That's pretty hard work. Blow it out, and it stunk. 7th floor, and old floor. Quite a job making one of these farms out of that. And you can see over there, it on that hill, them big stumps, there's a lot of them over there yet. You know, it's just a hard, hard way. Well, of course, now they got the dozers, not much of a job, you know, to clear a piece of that. But, well, you do it all with a team and powder, blocks and lines, that's different. And that's where we had to use it to clear it. Now, my wife and my triple boy, they cleared that flat over there with one horse. And the company, they had a nice little team up there, and one of them, he got sick and died.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And the dog, he come down, he said, Dick, don't you want a horse? And I said, what's the matter?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: He said, I'll give you a old rowdy. I said, you can use it down there. And I said, sure, I'll take him. So I went up and got him. Give me a set of harnesses and everything. So, of course, out there in the flats, you know, it's just small black pine trees. The old horse, he know more about the phone than my wife, and that cripple feller did. They come down the track from work, you know, and the old horse, they'd have the old horse out there, and he'd try to stump one way, and then come in, back up, and try it the other way. He'd work around, they got a little, of course, it was just a little black pine. But, gosh, he knowed how to pull, and I knowed how he did. Yeah, I think that's the way they cleared that back.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did you go about getting the big stuff? Did it take a real long time to clear an acre of that?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Yeah, well, I'll tell you, you know, I had a little... What I would do with that engine, I'd load my whole one up and load it. And I thought I had a big choker that come off a ground line, don't you, the tag line, what we called the tag line was 90 feet long, an inch and a quarter cable. And I'd hook that around it, and hook that engine onto it, see, when I'd get that engine to rocking, you know, you can rock the steam engine. The steam don't quite go over, well, it's up there in rock, you know, and back like that fuse. Boy, when that fuse went off that old stump -stump size house. Come right up out of the ground, and I don't need any of that right off. And that's the way I got a lot of them out of the platter where they just kept laying.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: That's the only way you're working things, I guess.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So then after you'd get the stumps out of it, what would you do?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Would you plan it to... I'd throw them all the way in and burn them, because I couldn't pass away. Well, after I got the dough here, I had to pile some of them yet and burn them. Because, well, well, them old timer -racking, they don't burn too good. They have a lot of timer -racking. Of course, now the red fire burns good, but... The old timer -racking doesn't burn at all, hardly. Would you then go and...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the first thing you'd planned when you planned?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well, in general, planned it notes. We had cattle here, me and I. We had cattle here, and we'd write it notes and cut it for hay. I'd really like to start talking about the IWW and the labor disputes in the camps.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well, they told them what they wanted. They come in and they wove them, and I told them what they wanted. Well, they wouldn't give it to them, of course. Well, they told them they was going to strike, and they did. Now, we had about, I guess 140, 50 men for breakfast. We had 90 for dinner. Except for we had 28. That's how fast it went out. And all the 28 that stayed, they stayed. We didn't... None of us go out because... Well, they was running three donkeys. Four, part of the time. And the boss, he had to go out and help to run two. He had this thing ringing on the donkey for about a week and a half, I guess.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well... Well, why did the guys...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who were these 28 that didn't go out?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well, I'll tell you. Herschel, Tribble and I, there were two of them. And of course, the foreman. He stayed. Well, they didn't hardly believe in striking. They figured there'd be another way of settling, I'll say. Well, they didn't go out. We didn't go out. And why I didn't go out, because... Well, I was in debt on the place, and I bought off the company, and decided to help them keep the folks. And so was Herschel Tribble. He was helping keep his mother. And there were one of them. He worked with me there in the kitchen two weeks. He had his card. I seen it. He showed it to me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who did?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: It's like W .W.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And he was working right there with me, and he said, and he said, you're going out?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: And I said, I can't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He said, why not?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: And I told him, and he said, don't blame me. I wouldn't be there. He said, I used to help keep my folks.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He said, you're not telling me a thing?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: I know just what you're talking about. And he said, you don't have to go out, pal. There was something else that didn't come up. Well, here they come from the hostels, you know, the sheriff. I don't know how many of them. Oh, there were four or five of them packing guns. They gave us all permit to pack a gun. And so, at all times, we left camp. We always had a gun on us, so we packed a round on us sometimes in camp. Oh, they come around there and shoot overnight, you know. And I put a fella in there, Brooks Mackey from Colfax. He had a store, and he had a little office building set right down the edge of the camp. It was in the main line of Milwaukee. And, boy, I'll tell man you never got by that board. Dear God. Herschel and I used to come in overnight. He'd come right out and stop us. Of course, as soon as he found out, we went out on our way to it. He'd stop us from wanting our business.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: They didn't want more money. All this truck for better conditions. You know, before that time, we all packed our blankets, pit bedbugs, graybacks and all that stuff. Well, all they wanted. They didn't want to get out of this packing their blankets and have beds.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where is it?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well, I come down home, and I had to get out some wood. And I told Dad, I said, you stay in camp. And I went to call the wood out, two cars over the wood. Went back up, and a monkey drowned. The mow hills were late, and I went out to camp. Boy, going up the road, tracking ahead, a motor runner and all that. Well, once a speeder or somebody gets hurt or something, they got a speeder up getting them out tonight. Got up there, and boy, the whole field site was lit up. Now I went on in, and I had a new camp. It wasn't a new camp, but they padded it all over and put in steel bunks for us. They furnished us our bedding, blankets, pillows, sheets, everything. And them sheets were pulled off once a week and sent in to laundry. No more packing your sugars.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Yeah, they had quite a time over that. Boy, they call it cobra. I blame them. Of course, I didn't help many with it, but I enjoyed what they done.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were most of the guys local guys, or were they just guys living in the logging camps who didn't live around here?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well, I'll tell you. It was in the IWW. Yeah, you know, but this way, the lumberjack was never safe in the Union.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know that, didn't you?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well, he was. Minnesota, West Con, all that, everyplace. Yeah, you just meet them from everyplace, working in the woods, you know. Get talking to them, work with them maybe two or three days and get talking. Minnesota, maybe Michigan, Wisconsin, been working in the woods there. Well, these fellas here, you know, they worked here all the time. And of course, a lot of them drifted here and went back there and went to work too. But, here was the warehouses, you know, they brung lots of their main men with them when they come out here.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: All their main men, they brung them. But of course, after a year, a few years, well, then they got people, fellas right around here. They'd take over, run their camps and such as that. So, that's the way that worked out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, do you know if many of the, did many local boys get into it, into the IWW and into the strike? Or were most of those the guys that kept on working?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: No, there wasn't many of the local guys got in. Of course, now here we'll talk about local. Well, I guess we could figure Spokane being local. Because, you know, that was the headquarters for the Rumba Jack. That's where he got a little steak. That's where he went, Spokane, blow in. You know, you should get tickled at them, you know. They'd say, I'm gonna quit. Oh, he'd probably have a hundred and fifty, maybe two hundred dollars coming. I believe we'll quit.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where you going?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Oh, he said they're playing two games a day more over at Rutledge, at Blackwell. That's over around Clark. Well, he'd drag his time and he'd go to Spokane and blow it. He'd come back and he'd work for Blackwell or Rutledge, maybe, oh, six weeks. And then get his money again, go back in Spokane. Come back up and he'd come back to where he started from.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I said, what's the matter?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: I thought you'd get more money over there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, he said, well, what?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: But the damn chuck, I just couldn't beat it.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: So there you go. That's the way they worked. All the old lumberjacks, you know, that is. What I mean, a single, young lumberjack, you know, of course. Herschel and I, we had our own bottom round bovial there at our home. And the two tanks, the regular lumberjacks. Of course, we stayed around and worked out at home all the time. Boy, they traveled from here and there and all over again.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Many of them ever settled down in here?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Oh, yeah. A lot of them. Now, you know, I know several that come down in here and bought places that I used to work with up around Mobile. And bought places, now they've sold out. I don't know where they went down.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But, there was one fellow, did you ever read that tall timber?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Yeah. Tree's Goutal. Yeah, a tree's Goutal.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That lumberjack preacher?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: I knew him well, Dick Perle.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was he like? What was he like?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well, they were just as nice a man as you ever met them. But don't sass him. One time, you know, he'd come out, first time I met Dick was, I was working in the kitchen. He'd come in there and I didn't know who he was. He talked to her a triple while, her little brother, he would cook and he'd talk to anybody a while and come on back to me. And he said, I'm going to give a sermon here after supper. So he was joshing and talking on and on and on. He said, I'll help you get the dining room ready after supper. See, that was my job then, to get the dining room ready for breakfast. Set the table all up. So, yeah, man, I had a couple of the boys there that didn't have to suit him. Well, they had no business. He's a preacher in the gospel. They had no business. But it kind of poked a light at him a little bit, you know, and he told them boys, he said, I'm giving a sermon here and I'd like for you to sit and listen. If you will. He kept on. Directly, boy, he went back there and he was a well -built man. He got one by one collar and one by the other and boy, he popped their heads together and drug them both out to the door and shoved them both out there and onto the walk. He prodded them right on down and when I got to the walk, he said, boys, now when you can listen, come in and behave yourselves. He come in and he said, I'll preach the rest of the sermon. They said, I'm going back in and finish it now. He did, he come back in. Come find out, you know, he's a prize fighter. Yeah, he told me, he said he was raised in Chicago across the track. And he said, I took up prize fighting. And he said, they got a hold of me. Well, he said, I'm working right up, he said, for lightweight champion. And he said, this minister got a hold of me. And he said, he changed my mind all about it. And he said, I took up the gospel there and he said, I quit my fighting. But he knowed I think I ought to go out of the pool.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Dick used to come down here and visit with us after a man and I moved here. And he preached, I don't know, four or five funerals right here in Princeton. Yeah, I know Dick well.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: And one thing I like about Dick, you know, he went to Montana, Oregon, Washington, and all over. And you asked him about some old timer and he could tell you just about where he was at, where I seen him, where he had seen him last. So that was a great help. I like to talk to Dick on out of town.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well, Herschel told me that you could tell me pretty good about Potlatch Joe too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Potlatch Joe?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: I was sitting, I just went in the bunkhouse, I laughed. Not at the time because, well, I wasn't scared. I didn't know what to do. I went back and the boys was playing poker. And there was a couple of fellas come in the night before and Joe was playing hand and pay poker. And one of these fellas had quite a pot on the table. And one of these fellas, he pulled a gun, pulled out a gun. That boy had been cleaning them all day. These two fellas, you know, come in. And he pulled a gun and Joe just retched in under the gun and raked all the pot in. Shot in the pot.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: He couldn't talk to me because I didn't do that anymore. But that's just what he happened to me. Poor old Joe, I don't know. You know, he was a funny fella. The boy always got his money. Now, Tom Kelly, he was foreman. And one time old Joe had thirteen checks coming. Tom wouldn't give them to him. He'd give them to him about ever so often.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You see that?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: So, Tom, he was a funny fella. He had Joe come by. So I was scaling and he said, you go down and tell old Joe to come up here. I went down and told Joe, I said, Tom wants to see you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What does he want?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: I don't know. You go see him. He sent me after you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So, Tom, he went up and he said, Joe, he said, you've been coming off the job and you've been coming in early, haven't you?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Yeah, I'm down. Well, he said, here's one check. He said, you take that and go to Spokane and come back for work. Well, Joe, he took a check. But he didn't go to Spokane. What he done, Harsh was running a camp there at Derry. Poor potlatch. Old Joe, he went down there on stage about a month. Come back up and went to work. You know, so running the train crew that night, they come to us, tying up their six. And he said, you can't guess what happened there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I said, why?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: He said, old Joe rode down to Harsh's with us this morning on the train. He said, he went to work down there. He stayed down there a month, got a check. Come back up and went to work. But the poor old feller, you know, he'd get work. He'd get all his money playing poker. Yeah. And some of them fellers in them camps or territories could play poker better than they all fellers. Because there were two lads that lived at Hurtley Boys, lived in Moscow. And they'd come in right after payday, two or three days. And they'd say that payday, get that, just one. He'd say the next payday, and the next payday, soon he got the boys cleaned up. And they'd go out to go to the short laws. And I know I was talking to Jess, and his mother lived right close to Herschel's mother there in Moscow. So he'd come up there and worked there that time. We were there twice that summer in camp, and he went over and bought his mother a nice home there in Moscow and paid the cash for it.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: And last I know of Jess, he was in Durkin, sir, in Spokane and in the boob hall. That was quite a gambling joint. He was in there handling the teddy -boobs. So you know, he must have been pretty good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would most of the guys know well enough not to play with these guys that were real good?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well, you know, they had a way of doing that. I'll tell you what I seen him do one time. He worked in the kitchen with me the time I was doing it. And I went in, and in five days it snowed and rained, the boys never left the bunkhouse. And right after payday, I went in and of course it started here. There's one, two bits, four bits, dollar. He got down to the last bunkhouse this guy was in. I better tell that, so I used to go clear through there and wash it down there, you know. So I went in there one day and Jess, he was sitting there for you, and jumped up. Shocked hands with me. Boy, he said, this is the first hand I've had today. Well, the ban of passing was around $500 in the pot, and he opened it for the pie lover. And one of them stayed with him.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: So, so, Jess, I seen his hand, all he had was a pair of jacks. So, he said, you drawed them on quickly. I said, I never had a hand like this today. I said, well, a pleasure Jess, he said, I'm going to. So when this one feller stayed with me, he didn't draw no cards. Then Jess, he bet the size of the pot, roughed him out, see. Well, he, the other feller, he had to give up. And they never know whether Jess is bluffing or what. So, that all he had was a pair of jacks. So, Jess roughed in the money and made his card down.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And the other feller said, where's your opener?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Oh yeah, Jess said, I forgot. And he just turned over his pair of jacks, he said, that's open. And they all seen his pair of jacks, and he put them back in the rest of his cards and told them in a dap. And he got that money on a pair of jacks. Well, I, I wouldn't have that much guts. Yeah. That's where he got it, you know, and they never could tell where he had it or where he didn't have it. They didn't bother him, if he lost it. He never changed complexion or belt. If he lose it for a good pot, you never knowed it. Nobody knowed it. Or if he had a good hand, they never knowed it because all you can get is showed any money on him what he had to. Yeah, they never knew that. Yeah, they knew, get the other hand, they knew it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that, was that gambling frowned on? Was that gambling frowned on by the management? Or was it okay for the guys to gamble? Was it, was that gambling legal?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: No. You know, a summer feller over there in Moscow, he come over and they knowed to stop their gambling in camps. And Jones said, why, he want to stop it. He said, I want to kill him. Well, he said, it's unlawful. And I said, Jones said, I know it. He said, look here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: If you would come and camp, he said, and worked eight, nine months, what would you do for recreation?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: He said, tell me. He said, there's a boy that brings in a gallon of whiskey, let him drink her. He said, if they want to play card, I'll let them. Well, he said, if you talk like you talk, Jones said, there's no talking. But he never done it. Well, you know, if anybody showed up that you know who was, I went down to the camp and told them, boys better be careful. It might be something up. Boys, you go down through there and they be playing cards, you know, have a box of matches out. Yeah. Well, they couldn't do nothing. They could play for matches in front of them. Sure.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well, now, I had heard that they were throwing the IWW boys in jail. That was part of the, when the strike came out. Oh, I tell you, they got to jail for it and they built a bullpen for them. They took them out to the ballpark and the boys, they never come up into nothing. They didn't try to get away. They kept, well, I had to feed them. Sure. They didn't have no jails couldn't hold them. And so they had to carry the feed out, food out to them, you know. Boy, they, they were pretty good. They built a fire and cooked their coffee. Have their stools right there in the ballpark. Well, finally, they just turned them loose. Called it a day. Of course. Lots of them, I tell you, there's lots of, now, you can take out in the harvest field, you know, that poor. They claimed there's lots of IWWs, but, box of matches and a bundle of grain, you know. Throw a machine up. Well, maybe there's something to it, maybe there wasn't. I think lots of that was machine outfits doing. One of them was the IWW. And I think the same way of starting firing the wood. I think there's a lot of them just doing it for onerous. I don't, I didn't lay any of them on the IWW. I don't know if they were ready. Of course, go to bed, I ain't. I just don't have a thought that, uh.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they come out, did they come out into the camps and arrest them?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Oh, yes, they come out and try to. They'll come out, old Pat Malone, that Pat. Patty would come out and ran a herd once in a while. Gosh about her. Didn't bother old Patty any. But, uh, some of them, you know, they carried on and on and on. After we got the sheets and pillow -slaves and everything, and nice steel beds and bunks. We got a, here's 23 come in there one night, we did supper. It was dark. I wondered why he's doing it. Up there that time we thought maybe there was a fire or something, you know, trouble. Went out and boy, I stepped down between the cars in here with a soldier standing there. He had a rifle that was a little taller than his head.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I said, what? What's the matter now to myself?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: I went on in to the bunkhouse and boy, there it was, they're going through all the bunks.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So old Pat, he come in and he said, where's your bunk, Dick?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: I said, right there in the corner. And he said, it's in a bad shape yet. They tore it all up but never put it back. Old Pat said, Charlie Summerfield, he said, that bed there, he said, blown to friend of mine. I said, maybe you'd better fix that back the way it was, like you found it. Charlie Summerfield said, well I don't know Pat. I said, yes I do. He said, you do. I said, I know him. And he said, I know his leaps there. I said, you put that bed back like you found it. Summerfield did. Put it back and then he was going to take two. The two dads he was going to take was from Montana. And what they were doing, I was running the load with them and they was hooking for me, see. And they were loading on the cars.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And they said, do you know them Pat? Do you know them Dick?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: And I said, sure I know them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Because they had been hooking for me about six weeks and know them pretty well. We'd been a chumming together.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He said, you ever hear them messing with my WW?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: I said, no I didn't. He said, they got a car. I said, that they have. They never showed it to me. So, Summerfield, he was going to take them from Moscow. Pat said, no you don't. Pat just even here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you got to get them? What do you got to prove?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well, Summerfield said, that happens about anything.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I said, what are you going to do when you get them over there?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: And they might turn around and say, hook you. Pretty deep or something like that. He said they could. So, Summerfield left them to say. Oh, just all this is asking out there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, the big thing with these guys is they was from Montana, right?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Yeah, these those cars from Montana, they ought to have cars. IWW cars, see.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: So, the boy is very big. I'm talking to him.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Oh, Pat, you know, he didn't care whether I WW or what or what. When I'm drinking a drink of whiskey, you take him to death. Oh,

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: yeah.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: You were telling me about some of those things Pat did during Prohibition that were pretty funny. Oh, you know, on time, I'll never forget that from long ago. We saw on logs, I can't date Herschel and I, we saw on logs. His cousin and I, one Sunday, we took an ocean called Potlice. Well, I suppose you better find anything down there to drink, Dick. So, we mucked around and they sent for about six gallons. Moonlight, moonshine, we got in the car and got up there on the hog meadows. Went across that bridge there. Boy, there's just glass like mud out in the middle of the road.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Cat said, what are we going to do?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: I said, we'll drive as far as we can. Going up to stop and see what's going on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So, what's that? Old Pat, he walked around and looked around the zoo and he said, is that you, Dick? And I said, yeah, why?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: He said, get the hell out of here. He said, we're looking for somebody to come through here with some whiskey. Yeah! He said, Summerfield right up there turning the road. He said, don't pay no attention to him, just drive on. So, we did, we just kept driving. So, I ducked in town, oh, about a couple of weeks after that. But, he said, come over here, Dick.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's the matter?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: He said, he's boarding there in the hotel, you know. He said, I just got some new furniture.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He said, why don't you come up and look at it? Went up there and you know what he had?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: A case, Canadian Club, that's never been opened. He said, let's open this and celebrate. And he said, listen, I'll tell you something.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You had whiskey the other night, didn't you? What? Did you ever know me to have a haul of whiskey?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: No, he said, I never did, but he said, I think you have. So, we even talked about having one of these cords. Called it good. Well, the old feller, you know, he, he wouldn't have turned anybody in. That'd have been his friend. Oh, I'd give him whiskey right down here. When he's wearing a star right here at Princeton. I'll give him drinks right there. And before five of them fellers there started rolling out, took him down to Thurg. Give him a drink. He said nothing about it. I was drinking and so was he and he ain't no rich and so was I. That's a big boy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, but what about Pat?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well, Pat, the way I understand it, Johnny McLeod, we had a whole team together. And Johnny knows Pat in the east, in Duluth, Minnesota. And how he come to get the beak off, why the niggas got into it. And they shot up one or two of the white lads. So, he went and one old Pat wouldn't go down and straighten him out. Well, he said he'd put on a star and go down and see what he could do. So, he went down and he killed three or four of the niggas. Right there. And brought the rest of them back up with him. And that's how he come to get the beak off. He come out here with them and he knows what he done there. Because Duluth, you know, is a big warehouse town. So, when he come out here, well, they put him on his devotee up there. You know, if he had to, he would. He could if he wanted to. So, that just kind of a man he was.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: A nice old poor, helped you out any way he could. He didn't think he could do anything for you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, who was he arresting during the prohibition? Did he ever arrest anybody? Who, him?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Oh, no, man. I tell you what he used to do with that bath in him. He used to, you know, the lumberjacks come in. The Milwaukee would run through the Elkhorn. And some of the boys get off the train pretty drunk. Pat used to go across the hotel, across to the depot. W -I -N -M Depot. And some of the boys would be pretty well loaded. And Pat, he'd look at them and he'd look up the street. And he'd look down the street and he'd turn around and he'd look back. And, in fact, he'd see them going way up that way. He wouldn't go over to the depot where the boys were drunk. The lumberjack.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: He didn't want to get me. Of course, I figured he'd pop me some of them and give them a drink, you know. Sometime or other. And he couldn't do nothing. He wouldn't. Well, he was kind of like T .P. Jones. He figured the lumberjack is no lumberjack himself. And he figured that they needed some recreation from some place. And he figured the way he liked his booze, he figured that's where they got it. Now, this little old farm is hard to beat.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Yeah,

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: and it's quite beautiful over here. Well, he took in a few, now and then. Very simple, he never rested anybody. Well, he never went after you. He'd just figure on going with him. Because he was going to take you one way or another. He had a back -y old head. He was a good -sized man, too. Big old Irishman. I guess he weighed around 260, 270, and a little over six foot tall.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, didn't Herschel have no pictures down there for you?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: I didn't ask him this time about them. He should have a lot of pictures. I don't know. Seemed like we had several taken together.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Clay, he didn't have any either, eh?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Clay did. I looked at some. Yeah, I know. I figured he would have. I didn't know where Carl was or not. Carl should have. Carl's got some, but they were going to get them out the next time I came back. I think he... Carl, you know, he was raised right here, and Clay, I think, was raised right there in Moscow. Yeah, and then he went out really young and told me.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Yeah, Clay, he's quite a lad. Now, you dig right in. There used to be about as tall a man as ever I saw.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You wouldn't have thought it, would you?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well, of course, I never learned it. One time I lived here, and that was when we had to come over the hill, the old road. And I had one of them mils superior, Chevrolet's five passenger. And it fell through ice, you know, and rock, and there she was. And I was going to get a fry pole and raise it up. Clay, he said, you can do that. And I went to hunt me a pole and come back, and he backed up to the spare tire behind it. Got ahold of it, and picked it up and set it up out of the ruts.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: So it wasn't long till I seen a potboat and his brothers in a rain barrel.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Yeah. And then a little later on up here, him and the sailor fell out one day, and they were going back up after dinner. And they were arguing over scale. And Clay said, God damn you, he said, if you keep on going to put you over there in that hoe pen. Crane was moving right up on the hoe pen. And he said, I don't think you can, Mr. Benson. He said, you set up like a shoe thing. He picked him up, and the train was moving, and he just threw him to the table sticking all right over in the hoe pen. Off the train. And then, I told Clay, you might have heard of anything, but you damn pretty cured it. Gee, I never would think he has much of a temper. He seems like such a sweet -natured guy. Well, he is. But you get him riled up, he laughs all the time. You never know. And the fella and him kind of fell out there, and Clay, they lived over there with our neighbors. About a year after they moved away from here, it was up to the other camp. He said to the fella, he always kind of wanted to get a hold of Clay. Well, I kind of worked around there, and I got him to put on the gloves one night. In fact, we had three sets of boxing gloves there on the camp. Boy, he found out he had much business with Clay. Boy, he just hit him in the life, you know. He comes back at him and hit him again. He knocked everybody in the door and half way into the bunk.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: He's a good man. I wouldn't want nothing to do with him.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Boy, sounds like there's quite a bit of fighting going on. Oh, yeah, you bet. Sometimes, well, they just sit down and listen and not turn back and talk.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Yeah.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Hey, Reggie. It sounds like Guy could stay out of it if he wanted to, if he wasn't looking for it. Yeah, I gave him a test and worked as long as he got it. Yeah, after one time down there, some boys come up and pollution him and say, gypo him. Well, after supper, you know, this kid ever logged over. They do the work all over after supper what they're doing out there. There's four or five old jacks in there in the bunk house where I was. I had a bottom bunk.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So, sitting there and Jim looked over at me and he said, Dick, he said, what do you take for that bottom bunk? I said, what do you want with it?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: He said, I think I'll buy it and put you upstairs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I said, well, what's the matter?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: He said, I want to put a roll away right in where your bunk is over these damn fellers. He said, I'll have some place to put their logs.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well, you know, them old timers, you know, they didn't take much. Well, they kind of quit logging after supper. Boys, they got poking fun at them so much, you know, that's bad. It's just too much. They kind of learned court. You couldn't blame them boys. They're getting quite a kick out of it, I guess. But some of them old jacks, they wasn't getting much kick out of it because it worked in the woods a long time. And after supper, they liked to have a little piece.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did any good friends of yours ever get caught in the prohibition? Ever get in trouble?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: No, not that one. It sounds like it didn't get enforced very much. I'll tell you what I did do though. Old Pat come down and there's a feller down here that's on the way to the lower camp and there's a feller with a little bootlegging. He was bringing it over to camp once in a while and Pat come down from Bullville, come in the camp.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So we seen him and Pat come in and Pat said, Do you know where Francis Lynch lives?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: I said, yeah. I do.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Of course, I know that somebody had turned Francis in, you see. Right then. I said, I got to come down and find out about something. And I know what he meant. So I went and told one of the other boys and he goes down and told Herschel Tribble. And Herschel went up and told Lynch that old Pat was there and he better hide it. If he had any. Pat, he went up there in the morning and he wouldn't go that night and he wouldn't give him time to get rid of it.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: So next time I see Francis, he said, Old Pat went down and I said, you know, I was talking to old Pat the night before. And he said, Herschel come up and told me. And that's fine then. Because he didn't want to take him. He had good stuff and Pat noted.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: He said, no, they ain't never bothered him. Of course, old Pat went up the next morning and was going about, oh, it was better than three quarters of a mile right up through the woods, except where Francis was living. Old Pat, he went. I see him going up through the woods next morning. After everything was all straightened out, he had to rest that night, old Pat, because come down on the train, he had to rest before he took off. He probably went up to Francis and gave him a big water gallon and that was it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How about the men getting along with the foremen? Did they usually get along well?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Pretty good. Yeah, they got along with the foremen pretty good. Yeah, of course, if they have any. I'll tell you about the lumberjack. If he had any difficulties too much, he wouldn't say much to anybody. He'd quit and go someplace else. Because anything didn't do him just right. He'd just walk off, drive his time and there he'd go. So, that sometimes I always figured would save trouble, you know, more or less. Well, a lot of them fellas, you know, they didn't care for nothing until they got started.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did the rubi all go along with them?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was Naga like as a supervisor?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: He was as good a man as ever I worked under. I don't care who he was or what he was. I worked under him a lot. All he asked you to do was what he told you to. And that was it. And if he'd done... Now, you know, I'll tell you one thing that happened right there at Five Nights. He took me down to unload trucks. Took me down out of the woods to unload trucks. I was the first fella to ever load and unload truckload logs there.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: So, I heard him tell Jim O 'Connell, now he said, listen, I'm going to tell you something, I lived here. Now, he said, Jim, he said, when trucking's over this fall, I want you to keep Dick here. And give him a job all winter. He was close to home. And he said, then he'd be here next year for us, for you to unload logs. Oh, Jim said it will, well, I'd heard that. I heard it right there, standing there and heard the whole thing. Well, Jim come down and he said to me, he said, when they're going to be done hauling us, it's tomorrow, they'll finish her up. Well, he said, I guess that'll be all. I said, all right. Well, Nog will come down that afternoon, I told him. So, here comes Jim over there, he's a nogler on the landing, you see. And he said, no way. And I said, Jim, he said, the understanding was, he said, you used to keep Dick here and give him a job this winter at the mill.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He said, can't you do it?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well, he said, I don't know. He said, you better be finding out damn quick.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And Jim said, what did he do?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: He said, I'll take him back up to the woods. He said, he can always work for me in the woods, anything. He said, he can do anything. He can always work for him in the woods anytime he wants to. He said, I'll hire him if I have to lay off somebody else. And he said, if I have to take him back in the woods, you can get somebody else to unload your trucks next summer. I won't have him down here. Well, Jim said, we'll see what we can do. And so, next day I laid off. So, Jim, he come around and he said, well, come down Monday. Well, what they was doing at that time, you know, that was just after the Depression. That's when the first started up after the Depression. And to get us all down there, we'd have to go down there every morning and report. Maybe we'd work, maybe we wouldn't. So, I went down two mornings and worked. Went down the next morning and didn't work. The next morning I didn't work. I went to a swamper that was the superintendent's meal and I said, oh, I'm telling you something, Ben. I'm not coming down here every day to find out where I can work or not. Well, he said, I want to start a job or one role when I can work. I was coming down to find out. So, I said, you need me out here, you know, where I live. So, he sent a man next afternoon and wanted to know if I would clean up the meals. I hadn't studied jobs. So, I went down the next morning and told him, he said if he wants to go work, tell him to be down in the morning. So, I went down the next morning and I took the job in the meal, cleaning up, until all the way along in the spring and then they had to go up and get a lot of logs out of the upper pond and I went up and helped get them out. So, they started trucking and went back down those logs.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: I went down every morning to find out. You know, eight mile down there and eight mile home, you know, gas is scarce, cars are scarce and everything else. You couldn't afford it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did you make out during the Depression? How did you make it go?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: I cut my wood. I showed you a picture of that little steam engine. That made my living here for about four years and a half, five years. My wife and I and another lad down here, we cut, we had 400 and around 15, 20 cord cut there and flipped. And you know what, we realized out of that wood and money. Now we'll say money. Well, it's $23. We sold it for two and a half cord right on the ground. But $23 is all the money we got out of it. We took hogs and feed, flour, everything. But now them farmers down around Colfax and down in there, they didn't have no money either. And they was the ones that got the wood, but they hauled wheat up here first and flour. They take their wheat, get a chop and bring flour up there, beans, nice big fat hog. You know, that's the way we got to buy it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that how everybody was doing it? Just trading in?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Just trading in here. Now there's some, a family or two here, but well, they had the income, besides the wood. But I know how I left here one time, that little engine. I was gone six weeks and I cut 1 ,500 cord wood. That would pile it up for me.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Of course I didn't fit all I done with saws. They helped me. It wasn't how much wood it would cut, it was how much could we get to it and away from it. And getting it away from it is hard.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where were you cutting it?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: I was cutting over here on Flat Creek. That's when I done a big job and then I come back home here and then that winter I cut all the wood off this, and then I went back in the underage, you see. I cut all the small stuff off of that, you know. I had to get her up and then move in and saw it up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you were cutting all your own land? I?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: You were cutting all on your own land. No, I was cutting for everybody. They piled her up for me, I had 50, maybe 75, 100 cord. All kitted and decked up, I did move in and saw her up. Well, nobody had any money and all they all wanted was wood and all the trade in their woods for groceries and this and that. What they done, we all worked together. Well, they go ahead and get all that wood ready for men. I'd move in and they'd all come in and we'd all cut her up. Well, what I done, I took my share out of it, wood. I didn't get no money, all I got was wood. And I'll tell you, what happened here, I'll never forget that. The fella, he come up and I know he was down around the food side, sold him wood before. And he had a hole in the truck. Well, he weighed more than 700 pounds. And he said, asked me if one of the neighbors up here was home. And I said, I don't know, he said he wants this hog. And he said he's supposed to have a woodcut for me. And he said, you know him as well as I do. And if he ain't got the woodcut, he said, yeah, I just have a little torn chivalrous truck.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He said, if he ain't got that woodcut, he said, you got any cut?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: And I said, well, I got 60, maybe 80 cords cut up there on the hill. But he said, I can't get up there. And I said, yes you can.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I said, if you bring that hog back, well, I said, how much wood do you want sold?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: He said, I'll tell you what I'll do. If he ain't got that woodcut, he'll come back and fill my rack full of wood, quarter to them, and you can have the hog. In about 30 minutes, he come back with a hog. We backed in over there and unloaded it and went out. Of course, he couldn't get up the hill. He didn't have no chance, so I had a good team here. So I took one of the horses and pulled his truck up on the hill and we loaded it up. We got 27 gallons of water down in that hog. Well, the old putter's too that high, not wide, and they just took him out of the pea patch. See, he's bending the peas where they trashed peas.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many cords of wood did you give him, you think?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Oh, I'd say probably a quarter and a quarter, three someplace, three and a quarter, a quarter and a half. I never measured that. I just rolled on wood until he had loads, and he said, that's good. If you want more, come back and get her. I said, that's my agreement. He said, I told you I'd give you the hog for if you fill my rack full of wood.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How much wood would you take for cutting so many cords, usually?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well, as a rule, I definitely took a fourth. It was all piled, and of course I had to furnish piles and stuff, you know, keep the sawpiles up and everything. They all said, you know, you couldn't run an engine for nothing. No. So, that's the way it went, and then I got a fourth for carpeting.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where would you sell it? Where would you trade it? Over in Colfax or police?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well, when we got to cut that big bunch down, that all went down below Colfax to them farmers, them big farmers. That was kind of good. We had a whole bunch of it cut there. A fella come in there one day, and he said, I heard you had some mud and stuff.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: He said, on a bottle open, he put on four cords on his truck. Boy, he come back that afternoon.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's the matter, did you upset him?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: No, he said he wanted a little wood.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I said, what'd he do?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: He said, I sold that in Colfax.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I said, did you?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Yeah. So we put four more cords on him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And he said, how much of this you got?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: And he said, quite a bit in here. I said, yeah. Well, he said that, you know, a lot of the boys down there want wood. He was a Russian. And he said, I'll bring them all up and come in that morning out of ten trucks. Them Russians, little Colfax, Russian settlement. So that's about all we got done. That poor nun would load wood.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: They hauled us in wood, they hauled us in flowers, beans, me. Yeah, I didn't have, we had at that time, I had, I guess I had about 14 head of cattle. And I didn't have enough hay to run them. And boy, they hauled me up enough hay to finish running my cattle. Yeah, trading, no money. Well, they had more hay in the morning. They didn't have no money.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: So, that's the way we all got by, just trading. Trading.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Oh, you know, it might be them times coming again.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: And if they do, I don't know what some of the young hulers are going to do. Just how they will take it. You know, them depressions, they hit about every so often. And it's about time I think we'd have another. Been quite a little while, you know, since we had that one. Oh, we had lots of fun. Here on the creek, we'd have dancing, and we'd get old enough, we'd build a cake, and everybody'd build a cake, and somebody'd go trade some wood, and get some coffee, and bring a pound of coffee, and we'd go dance all night. Well, there was four or five of us in here, played the violin. We all had, we had a piano here, and we could dance here, and another neighbor over there, he had a place where you could dance, and blow our wings and dance. Yeah! Had more fun them times than they do now. Yeah. That's right. Just everybody know the other fella, and everybody had their money around. All they, all of us playing the violins, all we asked for was just enough money to get some strings if we broke one.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Somebody, he'd get a hold of a dollar or so, and he'd bring it, put it in the cake or strings.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why do you think that they'd have a hard time now?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Well, I'll tell you, these young people's not used to it. They never went through it. What they gonna do is they take their gas away from them and no tires.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: And no money. Now I don't think that many of these young fellas got much of it saved up. I think it's all automobiles.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now I think they have quite a little battle, don't you?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Yeah. I think so. I think so. They just, well, they wouldn't know how to meet. No, they wouldn't. Yeah, sir, they just wouldn't know how to meet it. There we go.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: We had eight times out in the creek here when they were in the break, huh?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: We all had a good time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: No money. It was, that's one thing I can say for Potlatch. I don't know how it'll be now, but I bought this, I had 120 here. And it was three years I never paid him a cent on it and never paid him any taxes. I couldn't. I didn't have it.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: And the fella, he come up and tried to buy it out from under me, see. So the secretary, he's a land man there in Potlatch, and he said, I don't know. He said, I'll wait and I'll let you know in a few days. So he come up. I seen him downtown, I know he was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And he said, do you want the place, Dick?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: I said, yeah. I'd like to have it, but I said, we're behind on taxes and everything, payments. Well, I got a chance to sell it. And he said, I figured if you didn't want it, why I would sell it. If you want it, I'll hold it for you.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: So, he did.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: But, when I first started downloading logs down there, he get down and he's there. I think I bought first month. And he come down to my name one day and he said, Dick, he said, you know, you can start paying a little longer on the place. Well, I said, I think so. I figured on it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And he said, how much you figure you pay?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: I said, maybe I pay $50 a month. Well, I was only getting 49 cents an hour for working on the landing. And he said, I told him, I said, now what we say, I'll try $50 a month. I believe that can pull it. And, I don't know, that's quite a bit. I said, I'll try it. And he said, if we can't get the oil cut down, I guess. Yeah, he said, you do that. So, I figured. I paid $50 a month to catch up. Yeah, that's just what I paid him. $50 a month while I got caught up. I was getting 49 cents an hour.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know, that counts up slow, don't it?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Yeah, it counts up real slow. Well, I just wondered what some of them boys, that's making $50 an hour, have to go back to 49 cents an hour. And then he told me that he, that he, he found out that he hadn't been paying. No, they hadn't paid us either. He could have checked for $11 ,000 over there for back taxes. Of course, I was in Moscow. They haven't paid up on their land either. Oh, damn, bedtaps are on it. He told me afterwards that you can't say that. We didn't. They didn't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And what's this about, did you go over the mountain?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Yeah, they, uh, deeded that all over to, uh, the university. Rather than to pay all that rough hill there, you see. Yeah, they deeded that all to the university, rather than to pay taxes on it. They had the good off of it. They had all the cedars. And there's two pieces of land in there that they didn't own. Great Northern owned a half section, I think. There was a section in there. And the following half potluck, he owned 160 acres in there. The rest of it all belonged to company.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you say that everybody cooperated pretty well, though, before the Depression?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge
words: I mean, it seems like they were ready for it when it finally hit. Well, we hadn't ready, no. Well... But we had to be. Let's put it that way. The government was coming, and we noted it there. But of course, you know, them days, the people are not, uh, they went a little different from being away, a different way of getting around than they do nowadays. You know, now, you got to go to Spokane to meet your closest neighbor. We're in Moscow. You go home, go down and see him, and when we're home, we'll be gone, the car's gone someplace. Well, them days, you know, we'd all, I don't know, just like that, all together. But nowadays, people I know nowadays, well, let's put it this way. They've drifted apart to what they used to be. You know, we first come here, well, everybody was, you know, right together. But nowadays, yeah, it's different.
